data.description:
There are two options - first, we could create a datauri on the front end and send it to the server to send to printful. 

Second, we could send just the text from the front end, and create a PNG on the server, then send it to printful.

Ideally, we'd sign requests to Printful and make requests on the client side, but I'm not sure their API allows this.
data.title: Plan how to process images and send the to printful to create products

data.body: Decision locked (via conversation with human, 2026-05-05): server-side SVG rendering. Client sends {text, middletext}; server renders the print-quality PNG from a Go text/template SVG using resvg-go + the embedded Helvetica-Black.woff2; server hosts the PNG at a hash-keyed URL and gives that URL to Printful. Rejected: (a) client dataURI upload — loses determinism, browser-dependent canvas resampling, untrusted client bytes for fulfillment, multi-MB upload payload. (b) client-side direct Printful calls — Printful uses bearer tokens with no public-client signing flow, can't expose tokens safely. The one real loss is WYSIWYG between browser preview and printed file; mitigated by sharing the same SVG template between client and server so drift is bounded.

Scope narrowed (via conversation with human, 2026-05-05): defer all Printful API calls. This task now covers only: client sends {text, middletext} on 'Buy Shirt' click, server renders the PNG and saves it. Printful integration (mockups, sync products, orders) becomes a follow-up task. The render pipeline design (SVG template, resvg-go, hash-keyed files, Helvetica-Black.woff2 embedding) stays — what changes is the trigger (button click instead of speculative API endpoint) and the destination (disk save instead of Printful POST).

Folded into TYB-5 as one implementation unit (per human direction, 2026-05-05). The plan at .lattice/plans/task_01KQV5WQVAVKRHMKT5B5VA3BSH.md is preserved as the design doc; TYB-5's implementation sub-agent reads both plans. Cancelling rather than running a parallel lifecycle on a deliverable that's already done.
